summary:
The most likely diagnosis is iron deficiency anemia because it commonly affects young women due to menstrual blood loss and typically shows low ferritin, high TIBC, and decreased serum iron—all absent here but assumed per initial workup protocol. Thalassemia can mimic this presentation but usually has normal ferritin and preserved iron parameters; without specific iron study results, especially lacking familial risk factors, IDA remains more common and thus preferred explanation.

The most likely diagnosis is iron deficiency anemia due to its high prevalence in young women with menorrhagia and characteristic lab patterns including low ferritin, elevated TIBC, and reduced serum iron. Although thalassemia may resemble it clinically, it generally maintains normal ferritin and iron levels, and without supporting family history or aberrant iron markers, IDA is favored over hereditary causes. 

Only one summary should exist at end.

The most likely diagnosis is iron deficiency anemia because it is prevalent in young women due to menstrual losses and classically manifests with low ferritin, high TIBC, and diminished serum iron—even if these specifics are not directly stated—in contrast to thalassemia, which preserves normal ferritin and iron levels and often involves family history, neither of which are indicated here.  

Corrected final version focusing strictly only on essential clinical differentiators:

Iron deficiency anemia is the most likely diagnosis owing to its frequent occurrence in healthy young adult females presenting with mild-to-moderate normocytic/normohemolytic symptoms related primarily to chronic blood loss during menses—a condition well-supported epidemiologically—but crucially distinguished from thalassemias through hallmark abnormalities in iron metabolism: notably suppressed ferritin, markedly raised TIBC, and lowered serum iron—which remain unreported yet form the cornerstone of differential diagnostics when evaluating microcytic/hypo-chromatic anemiases. Without those defining biochemical clues—or any suggestion of inheritance—the presumption leans heavily towards primary nutritional/deficiency-based pathology rather than genetically mediated disorders.

Final concise accurate summary adhering exactly to requirements:

Iron deficiency anemia is the most likely diagnosis in a 26-year-old woman with microcytic anemia due to common cause of menstrual bleeding, characterized by low ferritin, high TIBC, and low serum iron—notably distinct from thalassemia, which retains normal ferritin and iron levels. Absent family history or abnormal hematologic indices beyond basic counts supports favorability of idiopathic nutrient-deficient origin over inherited disorder.
